lesson.title: Unlocking print() power-ups
masteryOutcomes: Understand that printing is always on a new line
Print multiple text and numeric values using the + and * operators.

concept: Combining values in print(): commas, the newline behavior, and the + and * operators on strings

explanations.text: You already know print shows one thing. Now the power-ups: put commas between things and print shows them all, like print("Score:", 100). Every print starts a fresh line, like hitting Enter after each one. And + glues two texts together while * repeats a text — "ha" * 3 makes hahaha.

print() accepts several arguments separated by commas and displays them on one line with a space between each, so print("Age:", 25) shows Age: 25. Each print() call ends its line, so two prints give two lines. Separately, + joins two strings into one — "Hello, " + "Ada" makes "Hello, Ada" — and * repeats a string a number of times — "-" * 5 makes "-----". One rule: + only joins string with string, so to glue a number onto text you write str(25) first, or just use commas instead.

When print() receives multiple arguments, it converts each one to text and writes them separated by a single space (the default separator), then writes a newline character — that newline is why every print starts a new line. The operators are different: + is string concatenation and produces a brand-new string, but both sides must be strings, so "Age: " + 25 raises TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str — fix it with "Age: " + str(25). The * operator repeats a string: "ab" * 3 evaluates to "ababab". Commas convert for you automatically; + and * give you exact control over the resulting string with no automatic spaces.

hintLadder.text: You want several values in one print — what single character separates arguments inside the parentheses?
Remember: text needs quotes, numbers don't. Check each value inside print() — is each one the right kind?
If you're gluing with +, both sides must be strings. Is there a number that needs str() around it, or could a comma do the job?
Run it and read the output line by line — commas add a space for you, + does not. Which one gives the spacing you want here?

misconceptions.trigger: Writes "Age: " + 25 and expects it to work
misconceptions.correction: + only joins string with string. Either convert the number first — "Age: " + str(25) — or use a comma, print("Age:", 25), which converts it for you.

misconceptions.trigger: Expects two print() calls to appear on the same line
misconceptions.correction: Every print() ends its line, like pressing Enter. Two prints always give two lines; to keep values on one line, put them in a single print separated by commas.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks 3 * "hi" multiplies math-style or should error
misconceptions.correction: With a string, * means repeat: "hi" * 3 (or 3 * "hi") makes hihihi. It's repetition, not arithmetic.

misconceptions.trigger: Adds manual spaces with commas, e.g. print("Name:", " Ada") and gets a double space
misconceptions.correction: Commas already insert one space between values, so don't add your own. Write print("Name:", "Ada") and let print handle the spacing.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes commas and + are interchangeable and produce identical output
misconceptions.correction: Close, but not identical: commas add a space between values and accept any type; + adds no space and requires strings on both sides. Try print("a", "b") versus print("a" + "b") to see the difference.

checks.prompt: Write one line that prints: Score: 100 — using a comma to combine the text and the number.
acceptanceCriteria: Passes two arguments separated by a comma: a string and the number 100 (not in quotes)
checks.passFeedback: Exactly right — the comma joined text and a number in one line, and print added the space for you.
checks.failFeedback: Check three things: one print, a comma between the two values, and 100 without quotes so it's a real number. Then run it.

checks.prompt: Write two lines: first print a divider made of ten = signs using *, then print the word Welcome joined from "Wel" + "come".
acceptanceCriteria: First line uses string repetition, e.g. print("=" * 10), and outputs ==========
Second line uses + to join two string pieces and outputs Welcome
The two results appear on two separate lines
checks.passFeedback: Beautiful — * built the divider, + glued the pieces, and each print took its own line.
checks.failFeedback: Almost — make sure the = is in quotes times 10, and that + has quoted strings on both sides. Run it and compare the two output lines.

practice.prompt: Make a tiny score banner: line 1 is a divider of twelve - characters (use *), line 2 prints your name and the number 100 in one print using a comma, line 3 is the same divider again.
practice.starterCode:
# line 1: divider
# line 2: name and score
# line 3: divider
acceptanceCriteria: Uses "-" * 12 (or equivalent string repetition) for both dividers
Middle line is one print() with a string and the number 100 separated by a comma
Runs without errors and produces exactly three lines of output

practice.prompt: In your own words: why does print("Age: " + 25) fail, but print("Age:", 25) works?
rubric: Says + requires strings on both sides (mixing string and number raises an error)
Says the comma version works because print converts each argument to text itself
Bonus: mentions str(25) as the fix if you still want to use +

options: an error, because you can't multiply text
practice.explanation: * repeats a string with no spaces added, so "ha" * 3 is the single string hahaha. Spaces would only appear if they were inside the quotes.

pedagogyRules: Teach one power-up at a time — commas, then the newline behavior, then + and * — and have the student run code after each before introducing the next.
When the student hits the TypeError from string + number, celebrate it as a discovery: read the error aloud, then guide them to str() or a comma — don't just hand over the fixed line.
If the student says they already know print, respect it: skip re-explaining and go straight to the closed check on "ha" * 3 or the string+number trap to verify, then advance.
Contrast, don't lecture: ask the student to run print("a", "b") and print("a" + "b") side by side and tell YOU the difference in spacing.
If the student asks about loops, input(), or f-strings, give a one-line honest answer, promise it's coming, and steer back to finishing the banner exercise.
Keep every code moment in the app: 'type it in the editor and press Run' — never confirm output the student hasn't actually run.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success after the student runs the code and the Output panel matches the target exactly — including spacing and line breaks. print("a"+"b") showing ab is NOT correct if the task asked for a b.
If the output shows TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str, name that exact cause — a number on one side of + — and never invent another reason like missing quotes elsewhere.
If the student is lost, drop back to one value in one print, get a clean run, then add exactly one power-up (a comma) and run again. One new idea per run, one hint per attempt.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student frustrated or wants to skip
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Acknowledge the feeling in one sentence, shrink the task to a single guaranteed win — print("=" * 5) — let them run it, then rebuild from that success rather than pushing the full exercise.

chunks.text: print() can display several values at once: separate them with commas inside the parentheses, and print shows them on one line with a single space between each. Commas accept any mix of types — print("Score:", 100) outputs Score: 100 — because print converts each argument to text automatically. Don't add your own spaces around commas or you'll get doubles.

Every print() call ends by moving to a new line, like pressing Enter. So print("one") followed by print("two") produces two lines. If you want several values on the same line, put them in a single print separated by commas — separate print calls can never share a line by default.

The + operator joins (concatenates) two strings into one new string with no space added: "Hello, " + "Ada" makes "Hello, Ada". Both sides must be strings — "Age: " + 25 raises TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. Fix it by converting the number with str(), as in "Age: " + str(25), or by using a comma instead: print("Age:", 25).

The * operator repeats a string: "ha" * 3 makes "hahaha" and "=" * 10 makes "==========" — great for dividers and banners. It works with a string and a whole number in either order. Worked example: print("-" * 12) then print("Ada scored", 100) then print("-" * 12) outputs a three-line banner with the score framed by dashes.

celebration.headline: print() just leveled up in your hands.
celebration.recap: You combined text and numbers with commas, saw why every print takes its own line, glued strings with +, and built repeats and dividers with *.
celebration.unlocked: Next: variables — store a value once and reuse it everywhere.

summaryBullets: You can now print several values in one line using commas, mixing text and numbers freely.
You can now predict exactly how many lines your output will have, because every print() ends with a new line.
You can now glue strings together with + and convert numbers with str() when + needs them.
You can now build dividers and repeated patterns instantly with the * operator, like "=" * 10.
You can now choose the right tool on purpose: commas for easy spacing, + and * for exact control.
openingLine: Nice to see you back, {name} — that first print() you ran was just the beginning. Today we unlock its power-ups: printing several things at once, gluing text together, and repeating it — small tricks that already point toward {goal}. Quick warm-up: what do you think print("ha" * 3) will show?
bridgeFromPreviousLesson: Last time you wrote print("Hello, world!") and watched it appear in the Output panel — now we'll make that same print() do three things at once instead of one.

turns.text: Easy — print("Age: " + 25) will print Age: 25. Plus just adds them.
I love the confidence — let's test it for real. Type it in the editor and press Run. What does the Output panel say?
It says TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str. So it broke?

It broke in the most useful way — Python is telling you + only joins string with string, and 25 is a number. You have two fixes: wrap it as str(25), or swap the + for a comma. Which one do you want to try first?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I even need this?

turns.text: Honestly, why do I need commas and + in print? One print worked fine yesterday.

Fair question. Real programs almost never print fixed text — a game prints "Score:" next to a number that changes every second, and commas are how you put those together. Want to build a tiny version right now?
In the editor, type print("Score:", 100) and press Run. What appears between the word and the number?

dialogueExemplars.title: Absolute beginner: I don't know anything about coding

turns.text: I have to be honest — I don't really know anything about coding. I'm nervous I'm already behind.

You're not behind — you're exactly where everyone starts. Coding is just writing instructions the computer follows one line at a time, and you already ran one yesterday. Ready for the tiniest possible step?
Type print("hi", 5) in the editor and press Run — just that. Then tell me: what showed up in the output?
It says hi 5. There's a space in the middle!
You just found today's first power-up all by yourself: the comma prints both things and adds that space for you. See — you're doing coding right now. Shall we try a second power-up?
